content.text: Security and Privacy NFRs: Why GenAI Is Different

Security NFRs for GenAI systems go beyond standard web application requirements because the data flows in these systems are unusual in two ways. First, user-supplied natural language prompts can carry sensitive information — names, emails, medical details — interleaved with legitimate queries. Second, the model itself is an untrusted execution environment: it can be manipulated by adversarial input embedded in external data it processes. Both properties require explicit NFRs that have no equivalent in traditional CRUD applications.
This document defines the security and privacy NFRs that belong in every GenAI system's architecture specification and shows how to implement the key controls.

attrs.alt: GenAI data flow security zones and controls

content.text: Every piece of data flowing through a GenAI system must carry an explicit classification label. Classification determines which controls apply, what the retention limit is, and whether the data can flow to external model providers.

import re
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import IntEnum

class DataClass(IntEnum):
    PUBLIC = 0
    INTERNAL = 1
    CONFIDENTIAL = 2
    PII = 3
    SECRET = 4

# PII pattern matchers (extend with NER model for production)
_PII_PATTERNS = [
    re.compile(r'\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z|a-z]{2,}\b'),  # email
    re.compile(r'\b\d{3}[-.]?\d{2}[-.]?\d{4}\b'),  # SSN
    re.compile(r'\b(?:\d[ -]?){13,16}\b'),            # credit card
    re.compile(r'\b(0[1-9]|1[012])[-/.](0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])[-/.](19|20)\d\d\b'),  # date of birth
]

_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
    re.compile(r'(?i)(api[_-]?key|secret|password|token)\s*[:=]\s*\S+'),
    re.compile(r'sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{32,}'),  # OpenAI key format
]

@dataclass
class ClassificationResult:
    label: DataClass
    matched_patterns: list[str]
    can_send_to_provider: bool
    requires_redaction: bool

def classify_text(text: str) -> ClassificationResult:
    label = DataClass.PUBLIC
    matched = []

    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
        if pat.search(text):
            label = max(label, DataClass.SECRET)
            matched.append("secret_pattern")

    for pat in _PII_PATTERNS:
        if pat.search(text):
            label = max(label, DataClass.PII)
            matched.append("pii_pattern")

    can_send = label < DataClass.SECRET
    requires_redaction = label >= DataClass.PII

    return ClassificationResult(
        label=label,
        matched_patterns=matched,
        can_send_to_provider=can_send,
        requires_redaction=requires_redaction,
    )

# Usage in prompt pipeline
result = classify_text("My email is alice@example.com, please summarize my contract.")
if result.requires_redaction:
    raise ValueError(f"PII detected ({result.matched_patterns}): must redact before sending to model")

Prompt injection occurs when adversarial content in user input or retrieved documents overrides the system prompt or extracts protected context. It is the most critical security threat unique to GenAI systems. NFRs must specify detection coverage and the maximum false-negative rate.
Injection detection coverage: must detect ≥ 95% of known prompt injection patterns (tested quarterly against OWASP LLM Top 10 test set)
False positive rate: must remain ≤ 2% on benign production traffic (measured via monthly sample review)
Latency budget: injection scan must complete within 50ms p99 (to stay within total latency NFR)
Scope: scan applies to all user-supplied text, all retrieved document chunks, and all tool call results before they enter context
Escalation: blocked prompts are logged with full text, user ID, and timestamp; security team alerted for patterns indicating targeted attacks
TLS 1.3 minimum; TLS 1.2 only with explicit risk acceptance
AES-256 (cloud default CMK or customer CMK)
SSE-C or SSE-KMS with per-tenant CMK
AES-256-GCM; separate key per data category
Automatic rotation every 12 months; immediate on compromise
FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or cloud HSM equivalent

No secret — API key, database password, model provider credential — may appear in environment variables, container images, source code, or log output. All secrets must be fetched at runtime from a secrets manager with audit logging enabled.

When a user prompt contains PII and the system must send it to an external model provider, the PII must be tokenized before transmission. The model receives a synthetic token (e.g. PERSON_001) and the mapping is resolved locally when the response returns. This ensures that PII never leaves the trust boundary.

import re
import uuid
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class PIIVault:
    # token -> original value, stored encrypted in a local cache
    _store: dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict)

    def tokenize(self, text: str) -> str:
        email_pat = re.compile(r'[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z|a-z]{2,}')
        result = text
        for match in email_pat.finditer(text):
            original = match.group()
            token = f"EMAIL_{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8].upper()}"
            self._store[token] = original
            result = result.replace(original, token, 1)
        return result

    def detokenize(self, text: str) -> str:
        for token, original in self._store.items():
            text = text.replace(token, original)
        return text

# Usage
vault = PIIVault()
raw_prompt = "Summarise the contract for alice@example.com"
safe_prompt = vault.tokenize(raw_prompt)
# safe_prompt: "Summarise the contract for EMAIL_3F1A2B9C"

# ... call model provider with safe_prompt ...
raw_response = "The contract for EMAIL_3F1A2B9C expires on 2027-01-01."
final_response = vault.detokenize(raw_response)
# final_response: "The contract for alice@example.com expires on 2027-01-01."
Embedding deletion on source doc deletion
Automated cascade delete across all stores
GenAI systems typically operate across multiple tenants or user roles. The access control NFRs must define the model of isolation and the minimum-privilege requirements:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class Role(Enum):
    VIEWER = "viewer"
    USER = "user"
    POWER_USER = "power_user"
    ADMIN = "admin"

@dataclass
class AccessPolicy:
    role: Role
    max_tokens_per_request: int
    max_requests_per_day: int
    can_access_audit_logs: bool
    can_export_responses: bool
    allowed_models: list[str]

ROLE_POLICIES: dict[Role, AccessPolicy] = {
    Role.VIEWER: AccessPolicy(
        role=Role.VIEWER,
        max_tokens_per_request=512,
        max_requests_per_day=50,
        can_access_audit_logs=False,
        can_export_responses=False,
        allowed_models=["gpt-4o-mini"],
    ),
    Role.USER: AccessPolicy(
        role=Role.USER,
        max_tokens_per_request=4096,
        max_requests_per_day=500,
        can_access_audit_logs=False,
        can_export_responses=True,
        allowed_models=["gpt-4o-mini", "gpt-4o"],
    ),
    Role.ADMIN: AccessPolicy(
        role=Role.ADMIN,
        max_tokens_per_request=128000,
        max_requests_per_day=10000,
        can_access_audit_logs=True,
        can_export_responses=True,
        allowed_models=["gpt-4o-mini", "gpt-4o", "o1-preview"],
    ),
}

def enforce_policy(user_role: Role, model_id: str, token_count: int) -> None:
    policy = ROLE_POLICIES[user_role]
    if model_id not in policy.allowed_models:
        raise PermissionError(f"Role {user_role.value} cannot access model {model_id}")
    if token_count > policy.max_tokens_per_request:
        raise PermissionError(
            f"Request exceeds token limit: {token_count} > {policy.max_tokens_per_request}"
        )

Every prompt sent to a GenAI system and every response received must be logged with sufficient context to reconstruct what happened during any security investigation or regulatory audit. The minimum required fields are:
request_id: globally unique, tied to the user's session and all downstream calls
user_id + org_id: who made the request
model_id: which model version processed the request
data_classification: highest sensitivity class in the prompt context
input_token_count / output_token_count: for cost attribution and anomaly detection
injection_scan_result: whether the scan ran, its outcome, and any matched pattern
response_hash: SHA-256 of the response text for tamper evidence

Do NOT log the full prompt or response text to general application logs. These must be written to a separate, access-controlled audit store with field-level encryption. The general log carries only the request ID and outcome so on-call engineers can triage without seeing user data.

# security-nfrs.yaml
service: genai-inference-api
version: "1.0"
last_reviewed: "2026-01-15"

data_classification:
  default_class: INTERNAL
  classifier: "regex+ner-model-v2"
  pii_block_action: redact_and_tokenize
  secret_block_action: reject_request

transport:
  minimum_tls: "1.3"
  certificate_rotation_days: 90
  mutual_tls_required: true  # service-to-service

encryption_at_rest:
  block_storage: AES-256 CMK per tenant
  object_storage: SSE-KMS CMK per tenant
  field_level_pii: AES-256-GCM separate key per category
  key_rotation_months: 12

prompt_injection:
  scan_enabled: true
  scan_latency_p99_ms: 50
  detection_coverage_target_pct: 95
  false_positive_target_pct: 2
  scope:
    - user_input
    - rag_chunks
    - tool_results

access_control:
  model: RBAC
  token_limits_enforced: true
  rate_limits_enforced: true
  cross_tenant_isolation: hard  # separate namespaces, no shared resources

audit:
  all_requests_logged: true
  log_store: immutable-worm
  retention_years: 7
  access_control: security-team-only
  fields_required:
    - request_id
    - user_id
    - org_id
    - timestamp_utc
    - model_id
    - data_classification
    - injection_scan_result
    - response_hash

provider_contracts:
  zero_retention_api_flag: required
  opt_out_training: required
  data_processing_agreement: required
  private_endpoint: required_for_confidential_plus
  audit_right: required_for_regulated_tiers

retention:
  pii_prompt_logs_days: 90
  general_logs_days: 365
  audit_logs_years: 7
  right_to_erasure_days: 30
Security Review Gate for Architecture Approval

The following checklist items are the minimum security NFRs that must be verified before any GenAI service receives architecture review board approval. Each item maps to the controls in this document:
[ ] Data classification scheme defined and enforced at prompt-assembly time
[ ] PII tokenization implemented for all external provider calls involving user data
[ ] Prompt injection scanning enabled on all user input and RAG-retrieved content
[ ] Secrets managed via secrets manager; no env-var or hardcoded credentials
[ ] Encryption at rest: CMK per tenant for CONFIDENTIAL and above
[ ] TLS 1.3 enforced end-to-end; mTLS between internal services
[ ] Audit log captures all 8 required fields; stored in immutable WORM store
[ ] Data Processing Agreement signed with all external model providers
[ ] Zero-retention API flag enabled for provider calls carrying CONFIDENTIAL data
[ ] RBAC policies defined for all roles; token and rate limits enforced
[ ] Retention and deletion schedule documented and automated
[ ] Right-to-erasure workflow tested with synthetic user data

Security and privacy NFRs for GenAI systems extend standard application security with three AI-specific requirements: PII tokenization before external model calls, prompt injection detection on all input and retrieved content, and audit logging that includes model, classification, and injection scan result alongside the standard request metadata. The data classification scheme is the load-bearing foundation — it determines which controls apply to each data element. Define it first, enforce it at prompt assembly, and verify it in every architecture review. The remaining controls — encryption, access policies, secrets management, and retention — follow standard enterprise patterns but must be applied consistently across the additional data stores (vector databases, prompt logs, model fine-tuning datasets) that GenAI systems introduce.
